title: Scaling Fermentation Batches
subtitle: How to confidently scale recipes up or down without losing quality or safety

sections.content: You've made a perfect quart of [[recipe:sauerkraut-classic]] and now you want to fill a 10-litre crock. Or you have half a cabbage and want to make a small jar. Scaling fermentation recipes is straightforward once you understand the core principle: it's all about ratios, not fixed quantities [cite:c1]. Unlike baking, where scaling can introduce unexpected complications, fermentation scales remarkably well — as long as you keep your salt percentages and brine concentrations consistent [[article:salt-and-fermentation]].

The single most important thing to maintain when scaling is the salt-to-vegetable ratio. Whether you're making 500 grams or 50 kilograms, the salt percentage must stay the same — typically 2–3% for dry-salted ferments (like sauerkraut) and 3–5% brine for brine-submerged ferments (like pickles) [cite:c2]. This ratio controls the speed of fermentation, the balance of microbial populations, texture, and safety [[article:ph-levels-fermentation]]. Change the ratio, and you change the entire fermentation dynamic.

sections.title: Never Scale by Volume for Salt
sections.content: Different salts have dramatically different densities. A tablespoon of fine table salt weighs roughly 18 grams, while a tablespoon of coarse kosher salt weighs only 9–15 grams depending on the brand. Always scale salt by weight (grams), never by volume (tablespoons). A kitchen scale is essential for accurate scaling [[article:salt-and-fermentation]].
When you increase batch size, the fundamental chemistry stays the same, but a few practical factors shift [cite:c1]:

items: **Fermentation speed** — Larger batches have more thermal mass and retain heat generated by microbial activity. They may ferment slightly faster than small batches in the same environment.
**Temperature management** — A 10-litre crock in a warm kitchen is harder to cool than a 1-litre jar in the fridge. Monitor temperature more closely with large batches.
**Packing and submersion** — Larger vessels require more attention to packing vegetables tightly and keeping them submerged. Invest in proper weights for large crocks.
**Mixing consistency** — When salting a large batch of shredded cabbage, it's harder to ensure even salt distribution. Mix thoroughly in a large bowl or tub before packing into the vessel.
**Vessel selection** — Mason jars work for 1–2 litres. For 5+ litres, consider a fermentation crock or food-grade bucket.

sections.content: Small batches are ideal for experimentation — trying new vegetables, spice combinations, or salt levels without committing a large quantity of ingredients. However, there is a practical lower limit [cite:c3].

items: **Minimum recommended batch**: About 250–500 grams of vegetables. Below this, it becomes difficult to generate enough brine to keep vegetables submerged.
**Small jar challenges**: In a very small container, the ratio of air (headspace) to ferment is high, which can encourage surface yeast and mold. Fill small jars as full as possible.

**Salt measurement precision**: At small scales, measurement errors are amplified. If you're off by 1 gram of salt in a 500g batch, that's a 0.2% error in salt concentration — enough to notice. Use a scale accurate to at least 1 gram.
**Vessel choice**: Half-pint (250ml) or pint (500ml) mason jars work well for small experimental batches.
**Scaling salt by volume** — The most common and most dangerous mistake. Always weigh your salt.

**Forgetting to account for add-ins** — If your original recipe includes 1 kg cabbage + 50g carrots + 10g garlic, and you double it, double everything — including the spices and aromatics, not just the primary vegetable.

**Using the same fermentation time** — A 10-litre batch in a warm kitchen may reach target acidity faster than a 1-litre jar in a cool basement. Taste and test pH rather than relying on fixed timelines [cite:c2].
**Insufficient mixing at large scale** — Unevenly salted vegetables will ferment unevenly, with some pockets too salty (slow fermentation) and others too fresh (risk of spoilage).
**Not adjusting vessel size** — Cramming a doubled recipe into the same jar creates overflow problems. Ensure your vessel has 10–15% headspace for expansion and CO₂ production.

sections.content: Use this table as a reference for scaling common ferments. The formula is simple: multiply the vegetable weight by the salt percentage to get the salt weight. For brines, multiply the water weight by the brine percentage.

sections.caption: Scaling worksheet: example calculations for sauerkraut (2.5% salt) and brine pickles (3.5% brine)

sections.title: The Universal Formula
sections.content:
Salt needed (grams) = Weight of vegetables or water (grams) × Salt percentage ÷ 100

Example: 2,000 g cabbage × 2.5 ÷ 100 = 50 g salt

This formula works at any scale. Memorize it and you can scale any fermentation recipe with confidence.

citations.text: Katz, Sandor Ellix. The Art of Fermentation: An In-Depth Exploration of Essential Concepts and Processes from Around the World.
citations.author: Sandor Ellix Katz

citations.text: Shockey, Kirsten K., and Shockey, Christopher. Fermented Vegetables: Creative Recipes for Fermenting 64 Vegetables & Herbs.
citations.author: Kirsten K. Shockey and Christopher Shockey

citations.text: Lewin, Alex. Real Food Fermentation: Preserving Whole Fresh Food with Live Cultures in Your Home Kitchen.
citations.author: Alex Lewin
